text: I'm 112 years old and I want to tell you
something before I run out of time. I've
watched almost everyone I ever knew
leave this world. My childhood friends,
my brothers, my wife, even some of my
own children. And the thing that haunts
me most isn't death itself.
It's watching people give up decades of
life because they didn't know something
simple. something I'm about to share
with you right now. Seven ordinary foods
kept me alive longer than almost anyone
on this planet. And I need you to
understand that this isn't about being
lucky or having good genes.
My father died at 59,
my mother  at 61.
Heart disease ran in my family like a
curse. But I'm still here, still
walking, still thinking clearly. And it
comes down to choices I made in my
kitchen, not a doctor's office. If
you're listening to this, you still have
time, but that time is shorter than you
think it is. When I turned 50, I thought
I had my whole life behind me. I was
already older than my parents ever got
to be. I figured whatever time I had
left was borrowed. My doctor told me my
cholesterol was dangerous. My blood
pressure was through the roof. He said
I'd be lucky to see 60. He gave me pills
and told me to take them forever. And
for a while, I did. But those pills made
me feel worse, not better. I was tired
all the time. My stomach hurt. I felt
like I was slowly fading away. So, I
made a decision that changed everything.
I stopped waiting for medicine to save
me. I started paying attention to the
oldest people I could find, the ones who
were still sharp and strong in their 80s
and 90s. And I noticed something. They
weren't eating what I was eating. They
weren't living how I was living. So, I
started changing things. Seven foods.
That's all it took. And here I am 62
years later, still here to tell you
about it. The first food sounds so plain
that people used to laugh when I talked
about it. Oatmeal.
I started eating a bowl of plain oatmeal
every single morning when I was 52 years
old. Not the sugary instant kind that
comes in little packets. Just regular
old-fashioned oats cooked slow with
water, a pinch of cinnamon, and maybe a
handful of raisins or berries if I had
them. My wife thought I'd lost my mind.
She said that's what farmers feed their
horses. But I kept at it. And I'm
telling you, within six months, my
cholesterol dropped so much my doctor
thought I was on some new medication.
When I told him it was just oatmeal, he
didn't believe me at first. But it's
true. Oatmeal has something called
soluble fiber. And that fiber grabs onto
the bad cholesterol in your body and
pulls it out before it can clog up your
arteries.
Every single morning for 60 years,
I've had that bowl of oatmeal. It became
my ritual, my foundation,
and I believe with everything in me that
it saved my heart. The second food is
something I eat every afternoon around
Walnuts, just a small handful, maybe
seven or eight of them. I started doing
this when I was in my early 60s because
I kept forgetting things, little things
at first, where I put my keys, what day
of the week it was, and it scared me.
I'd seen my uncle lose his mind to what
we now call Alzheimer's.
And I was terrified the same thing was
happening to me. So, I started reading,
asking questions, and someone told me
walnuts were good for the brain. I
didn't really believe it, but I figured
it couldn't hurt. And I'm telling you,
within a year, my memory got sharper. I
could remember conversations.
I could recall names. It felt like a fog
had lifted. Now I'm 112, and I still
remember the name of my third grade
teacher. I still remember the address of
the house I lived in when I was seven. I
think those walnuts kept my mind alive.
And in all these years, I've never
stopped eating them. The third food is
something I wish I'd started eating when
I was 20 instead of 50. Spinach and dark
leafy greens. I eat them almost every
single day now. Sometimes in a salad,
sometimes cooked with a little garlic
and olive oil. I'm not talking about a
tiny garnish on the side of your plate.
I mean a real portion, at least a cup or
more. When I was younger, I thought
vegetables were just something you
tolerated to get to the meat and
potatoes. But the truth is, greens are
what keep your blood clean, your bones
strong, and your body able to fight off
the wear and tear of living. I noticed
after I started eating greens every day,
I stopped getting sick as often. I had
more energy, my skin looked better, and
I felt lighter, like my body wasn't
working so hard just to keep itself
going. Your body doesn't repair itself
the way it used to when you were young.
Every bad meal adds up. Every toxin
sticks around a little longer. But when
you eat greens, you're giving your body
what it needs to clean itself out and
stay strong.
The fourth food changed how my body felt
from the inside out. Fish. Real fish.
Not fried fish sticks or anything
breaded. I'm talking about salmon,
mackerel, sardines.
The kind of fish that's rich and oily
and full of life. I started eating fish
at least twice a week when I was in my
mid50s and it made a difference. I could
actually feel. My joints stopped aching.
My knees, which used to hurt every time
I stood up, started feeling better. I
could walk farther without getting
tired. And my doctor told me my blood
work looked better than it had in years.
Fish has something called omega-3 fatty
acids. And those are like oil for your
joints and fuel for your brain. I know
fish can be expensive, but you don't
need fancy cuts. Canned sardines work
just as well. The point is to get those
oils into your body regularly. It's one
of the best decisions I ever made. The
fifth food is something I add to almost
everything I cook. Garlic. Fresh garlic.
Not the powdered stuff in a jar. I chop
up a clove or two and throw it into my
eggs, my soups, my vegetables, my fish.
It takes 30 seconds and it makes
everything taste better. But more than
that, garlic has kept me from getting
sick. I'm serious. I can't remember the
last time I had a bad cold or the flu.
Even when everyone around me was
coughing and sneezing, I stayed healthy.
Garlic is a natural antibiotic.
It fights off bacteria and viruses
before they can take hold. And it also
lowers blood pressure. My blood pressure
used to be so high the doctor said I was
a walking time bomb. After I started
eating garlic every single day, my
numbers came down. Slowly, steadily,
naturally, no extra pills, just garlic.
If I could go back and tell my younger
self one thing, it would be to start
cooking with garlic every single day.
The sixth food is small but powerful.
Blueberries. I eat them almost every
day, either fresh in the summer or
frozen the rest of the year. Just a
handful mixed into my oatmeal or eaten
plain as a snack.
Blueberries are packed with something
called antioxidants.
and antioxidants protect your cells from
breaking down. As you get older, your
cells are constantly under attack from
stress, pollution, bad food, just
living. And if you don't protect them,
they wear out faster. That's aging. But
when you eat blueberries, you're giving
your cells armor.
I've noticed my eyesight has stayed
sharp even at 112.
I can still read the newspaper without
glasses. I can still see faces clearly.
And I think the blueberries had
something to do with that. There's
research that shows they protect
your eyes and your brain. I don't know
if that's why I'm still here, but I'm
not  about to stop eating them
now. The seventh food is one that most
people ignore, and I think that's a
tragedy. Beans. Any kind. Black beans.
Pinto beans, lentils, chickpeas. I eat
them almost every day. Sometimes in a
soup, sometimes mashed up and spread on
toast, sometimes just heated up with a
little salt and cumin. Beans are one of
the most powerful foods on Earth. And
they're dirt cheap. They're full of
protein, full of fiber, and they keep
your blood sugar steady so you don't get
those crashes where you feel shaky and
exhausted.
When I started eating beans regularly,
two things happened. My digestion got
better. I'd struggled with constipation
for years and it just went away. And my
energy leveled out. I didn't have those
afternoon slumps anymore. I felt steady
all day long. Beans also keep your gut
healthy. And your gut is where most of
your immune system lives. If your gut is
strong,  you stay strong. It's
that simple. So, there they are. Seven
foods. Oatmeal, walnuts, greens, fish,
garlic, blueberries,
beans.
That's what I've eaten almost every day
for the last 60 years. And I'm not
special. I'm not some genetic miracle.
My family died young, but I made
different choices. Small choices,
consistent choices, and those choices
gave me decades that other people didn't
get. I've seen so many people die too
soon. And the hardest part is knowing it
didn't have to happen. I had a friend
named Harold. We grew up together. Same
street, same school, same everything.
But in our 50s, our lives split in two
different directions. I changed what I
ate. Harold didn't. He kept eating
burgers and fries and soda and sweets.
He'd laugh and say, "Life's too short to
worry about every little thing you put
in your mouth." And I never argued with
him. It was his life. But by 65, he had
diabetes. By 72, his heart gave out. And
I  remember standing at his
funeral thinking he could still be here
if he just changed a few things.
If he' just taken it seriously,
he'd still be here. I'm not telling you
this to scare you. I'm telling you
because it's the truth. The choices you
make today, the food you put on your
plate today, that's what decides whether
you're here in 20 years or 30 years or
40 years.  And I know it's easy
to think you have time. I thought that,
too. But time moves faster than you
think it does. One day you're 30 and
feel invincible. Then you blink and
you're 60 and your body is falling apart
and  you're wondering where it
all went wrong. Don't wait until you're
sick to start caring. Don't wait until
the doctor gives you bad news. Start
now, today, with one of these seven
foods. Just one, and then add another
and another. You don't have to be
perfect. You just have to be consistent.
I want to be honest with you. Changing
the way I ate wasn't easy at first. I
missed the junk I used to love. I missed
the sweetness, the grease, the
convenience of grabbing whatever was
fast and easy.
But after a few weeks, something
shifted. I started feeling better. I had
more energy. I slept better. My body
stopped hurting all the time. And once I
felt that difference, I didn't want to
go back. The junk food stopped tasting
good. It started tasting like what it
really was. Poison disguised as
pleasure. Now, the foods I eat every
day, those seven simple foods, they're
what I crave. They're what tastes good
to me. So, if you're worried that eating
healthy means suffering, I promise you
it doesn't. You just have to push
through those first few weeks. After
that, it gets easier and then it becomes
part of who you are. I've also learned
that you don't have to be perfect. I'm
not perfect. I still have a piece of pie
on my birthday. I still enjoy a cookie
once in a while, but most of the time,
maybe 90% of the time, I'm eating those
seven foods. And that's what matters.
It's not about one meal. It's about what
you do most days, most weeks, most
years. That's what adds up. That's what
builds a long life. And these foods
aren't just for old people. If you're 30
or 40 or 50, this is the perfect time to
start. The earlier you start, the more
years you get. I didn't start until I
was 52. And look how far it took me.
Imagine if I'd started at 25.
But even if you're 70 or 80, it's not
too late. Your body wants to heal. It's
designed to heal. You just have to give
it the tools. Let me tell you what my
day looks like so you can see how simple
this really is. I wake up and make my
oatmeal. I eat it slowly, sitting down.
No phone, no TV, just me and my food.
Around midm morning, I'll have a cup of
tea and maybe a piece of fruit. At
lunch, I make a big salad with spinach,
tomatoes, cucumbers, and some beans. I
dress it with olive oil and lemon juice.
Simple. Around 3:00, I eat my walnuts.
And for dinner, I'll have a piece of
fish with roasted vegetables, always
cooked with garlic. That's it. That's a
full day of eating. Nothing fancy,
nothing expensive, just real food
prepared  simply, eaten
mindfully. And that's been my routine
for six decades. It's not a diet. It's
just how I live. This is what lessons
from a long life is really about. It's
not about grand gestures or expensive
treatments. It's about the small, quiet
choices you make every single day. I
know some of you watching this are
thinking it's too late for you. Maybe
you're already sick. Maybe you've
already been given bad news. But I'm
telling you, it's not too late. Every
good choice you make helps. Every meal
matters. Your body is more resilient
than you think. I've seen people turn
things around in their 70s and 80s just
by changing what they eat. It won't fix
everything, but it will give you more
time. Better time. Time where you're not
in pain, not confused, not dependent on
a dozen medications.
Time where you can still enjoy life. And
isn't that what we all want? Not just to
be alive, but to actually live. So,
here's what I want you to do. Don't just
watch this and move on. Actually do
something  today, right now. Pick
one of those seven foods and add it to
your next meal. Maybe it's oatmeal
tomorrow morning. Maybe it's a handful
of walnuts this afternoon.
Maybe it's a spinach salad for lunch.
Just start.
And once that becomes normal, add
another one. You don't need to overhaul
your whole life overnight. You just need
to start because starting is the hardest
part. But once you start, momentum
builds and before you know it, eating
this way becomes automatic.
It becomes who you are. And if this
message meant something to you, I want
to ask you for something.
Leave a comment below and tell me which
of these foods you're going to start
with. I want to know. I want to hear
your story.
And share this video with someone you
care about. Maybe it's your mom or your
dad. Maybe it's a friend who's
struggling with their health.
Maybe it's someone who needs to hear
that it's not too late. Because the more
people who hear this, the more lives we
can change. And that's what this is all
about. Passing on what I've learned so
you don't have to learn it the hard way.
So you don't have to lose the years I've
seen so many others lose. I'm 112 years
old.
I never thought I'd live this long.
I've outlived everyone I started life
with. And when I look back, I don't
think about the money I made or the
things I owned.
I think about the mornings I woke up and
made that bowl of oatmeal. The
afternoons I sat down and ate my
walnuts.
The dinners I cooked with garlic and
greens and fish.
Those small unremarkable moments.
That's what gave me this life. That's
what kept me here. And I want you to
have that, too.
I want you to be around long enough to
see your grandkids grow up. To watch the
sun rise on a thousand more mornings,
to feel what it's like to be truly
healthy. Not just alive, but living.
And it starts with the food on your
plate. It starts today.
Don't wait. Please don't wait because
it's later than you think.
But it's not too late. Not yet.
You still have time.
Use it.
